title: IT Governance Fundamentals
description: Understand IT governance principles, structures, and the board's role in IT oversight

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IT governance ensures IT investments support business objectives and risks are managed appropriately. This is a foundational CISA topic closely linked to COBIT.

sections.title: What is IT Governance?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
IT Governance is the system by which an organization's IT is directed and controlled.

**Key Objectives:**

**Strategic Alignment:**
• IT strategy aligned with business strategy
• IT investments support business goals
• IT capabilities enable business needs

**Value Delivery:**
• IT delivers promised benefits
• Costs are optimized
• Value is measured

**Risk Management:**
• IT risks are understood and managed
• Risk appetite is defined
• Controls are appropriate

**Resource Management:**
• IT resources used efficiently
• Skills and capabilities developed
• Sourcing decisions optimized

**Performance Measurement:**
• IT performance monitored
• Metrics linked to strategy
• Improvements identified

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SAVR-P
sections.content:
**IT Governance Objectives: SAVR-P**

• **S**trategic alignment
• **A**ccountability (part of governance structures)
• **V**alue delivery
• **R**isk management
• **P**erformance measurement

(Some include Resource Management - SAVRP-R)

sections.title: IT Governance Structures
sections.content:
**Board Level:**
• IT/Technology Committee of the Board
• Overall oversight responsibility
• Major investment decisions
• Risk appetite setting

**Executive Level:**
• IT Steering Committee
• CIO and business executives
• Investment prioritization
• Policy approval

**Management Level:**
• IT Management Team
• Architecture Review Board
• Change Advisory Board
• Security Committee

**Key Roles:**

**CIO (Chief Information Officer):**
• IT strategy and operations
• Reports to CEO or Board

**CISO (Chief Information Security Officer):**
• Security program
• May report to CIO, CEO, or Board

**CTO (Chief Technology Officer):**
• Technology direction
• Innovation and R&D

sections.title: IT Steering Committee
sections.content:
**Purpose:**
Guide IT investments and ensure alignment with business.

**Typical Composition:**
• CIO (often chairs)
• Business unit leaders
• CFO or finance representative
• Major IT consumers

**Responsibilities:**
• Approve IT strategy
• Prioritize projects and initiatives
• Allocate IT resources
• Review IT performance
• Resolve cross-functional issues

**Meeting Frequency:**
Typically monthly or quarterly

**Audit Considerations:**
• Does the committee exist and meet regularly?
• Are decisions documented?
• Is business represented appropriately?
• Are metrics reviewed?
• Is the committee effective?

sections.title: COBIT 2019 Governance Objectives
sections.content:
**EDM Domain (Governance):**

**EDM01: Ensure Governance Framework Setting and Maintenance**
• Define and maintain governance framework
• Consistent with organizational needs

**EDM02: Ensure Benefits Delivery**
• Optimal value from IT investments
• Business cases and benefits tracking

**EDM03: Ensure Risk Optimization**
• IT risk within appetite
• Integrated with enterprise risk

**EDM04: Ensure Resource Optimization**
• IT resources adequate and used well
• Capabilities developed

**EDM05: Ensure Stakeholder Engagement**
• Stakeholder expectations met
• Communication effective

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IT governance ensures IT supports business objectives and manages risk
Key objectives: Strategic alignment, Value delivery, Risk management, Resource optimization, Performance measurement
Governance sets direction (evaluate, direct, monitor); Management executes (plan, build, run)
Structures include Board committees, IT steering committee, and management teams
COBIT EDM domain addresses governance; APO/BAI/DSS/MEA address management

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following BEST describes the primary objective of strategic alignment within IT governance?
options: Ensuring all IT projects are completed on time and within budget.
Ensuring that IT strategy is in line with and supports the overall business strategy and objectives.
Implementing the latest technological advancements regardless of business needs.
Minimizing IT operational costs through outsourcing and automation.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Strategic alignment focuses on ensuring IT strategy directly supports and enables the achievement of business goals. While the other options are important aspects of IT management, they are not the primary focus of strategic alignment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the 'big picture' purpose of IT within the organization.

reveal.question: Which governance structure is responsible for overall oversight responsibility and setting the risk appetite for IT investments?
reveal.answer: The Board Level, typically through an IT/Technology Committee, is responsible for overall oversight and setting the IT risk appetite.

rows.label: Focus
values: Strategic direction and prioritization of IT investments
Day-to-day IT operations and project execution

rows.label: Key Activities
values: Approving IT policies, prioritizing projects, and monitoring overall IT performance against strategic goals
Managing IT resources, implementing policies, and ensuring service delivery

rows.label: Members
values: CIO and business executives
IT Managers and team leads

title: IT Strategy and Planning
description: Learn how IT strategy is developed and aligned with business objectives

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IT strategy translates business objectives into technology direction. Auditors must understand how strategy is developed to evaluate alignment and effectiveness.

sections.title: IT Strategic Planning Process
sections.content:
**Step 1: Understand Business Strategy**
• Review business mission and vision
• Understand strategic objectives
• Identify business drivers
• Know competitive landscape

**Step 2: Assess Current State**
• Inventory existing IT capabilities
• Evaluate technology health
• Identify strengths and gaps
• Benchmark against industry

**Step 3: Define Future State**
• Determine required IT capabilities
• Align with business direction
• Consider emerging technologies
• Define target architecture

**Step 4: Gap Analysis**
• Compare current to future state
• Identify capability gaps
• Prioritize improvements

**Step 5: Develop Roadmap**
• Plan initiatives over 3-5 years
• Estimate resources and costs
• Define milestones
• Obtain approval

sections.title: Alignment Mechanisms
sections.content:
**Formal Alignment:**
• IT strategy derived from business strategy
• IT participates in business planning
• Common language and metrics
• Joint governance structures

**Continuous Alignment:**
• Regular strategy reviews
• Business participation in IT steering
• Project portfolio tied to business initiatives
• Benefits realization tracking

**Warning Signs of Misalignment:**
• IT initiatives not tied to business goals
• Business "shadow IT" proliferating
• Frequent project cancellations
• Low IT satisfaction from business
• IT seen as cost center, not enabler

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SWOT for IT
sections.content:
**SWOT Analysis in IT Planning:**

• **S**trengths: What IT does well
• **W**eaknesses: Where IT falls short
• **O**pportunities: External factors to leverage
• **T**hreats: External factors that pose risk

SWOT helps bridge current state to future state planning.

sections.title: Technology Roadmap
sections.content:
**Roadmap Elements:**
• Time horizons (1-3-5 years)
• Technology investments
• Application changes
• Infrastructure evolution
• Dependencies and sequencing
• Resource requirements

**Roadmap Considerations:**

**Retire:**
• Phase out legacy systems
• End-of-life planning
• Migration paths

**Maintain:**
• Keep current
• Security patching
• Limited enhancements

**Invest:**
• Strategic platforms
• New capabilities
• Digital transformation

**Emerging:**
• Monitor new technologies
• Proof of concepts
• Innovation agenda

sections.title: Auditing IT Strategy
sections.content:
**Audit Considerations:**

**Strategy Development:**
• Is there a documented IT strategy?
• Is it aligned with business strategy?
• Was business involved in development?
• Is it approved by appropriate levels?

**Content:**
• Are objectives specific and measurable?
• Is there a prioritized roadmap?
• Are resources estimated?
• Are risks considered?

**Execution:**
• Is the strategy being executed?
• Are initiatives on track?
• Is progress reported to governance?
• Is the strategy reviewed and updated?

**Outcomes:**
• Are benefits being realized?
• Are metrics tracked?
• Is the strategy achieving alignment?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IT strategy translates business direction into technology plans
Planning process: Understand business → Assess current → Define future → Gap analysis → Roadmap
Strategy includes vision, mission, objectives, initiatives, metrics, and roadmap
Alignment requires ongoing mechanisms, not just initial planning
Auditors evaluate strategy existence, alignment, execution, and outcomes

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in the IT strategic planning process?
options: Evaluating the current IT infrastructure's performance metrics.
Reviewing and understanding the organization's business mission, vision, and strategic objectives.
Conducting a SWOT analysis of the IT department.
Benchmarking IT spending against industry averages.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Understanding the business strategy (mission, vision, objectives) is paramount. IT strategy must be derived from and support the business strategy. Options A, C, and D are important subsequent steps, but they depend on a clear understanding of the business direction.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what drives the IT strategy.

reveal.question: Name three characteristics of 'Formal Alignment' between IT and Business strategy.
reveal.answer: 1. IT strategy is derived from business strategy. 2. IT participates in business planning. 3. Common language and metrics are used. 4. Joint governance structures are implemented.

rows.label: Nature
values: Structured and Defined
Iterative and Ongoing

rows.label: Frequency
values: Periodic (e.g., annual)
Regular (e.g., quarterly or more frequent)

rows.label: Focus
values: Establishing the IT Strategy
Maintaining Alignment and Adapting to Change

rows.label: Key Activities
values: Developing IT strategic plan, establishing governance
Regular strategy reviews, business participation in IT steering

title: IT Policies, Standards, and Procedures
description: Understand the policy hierarchy and how to audit IT policy frameworks

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Policies establish the rules; standards, procedures, and guidelines implement them. A solid policy framework is essential for consistent and compliant IT practices.

sections.title: Policy vs. Standard vs. Procedure
sections.content:
**Example: Password Security**

**Policy (What):**
"All systems must implement strong authentication to protect against unauthorized access."

**Standard (Specification):**
"Passwords must be minimum 12 characters, contain uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols, and expire every 90 days."

**Procedure (How):**
"To reset your password: 1) Navigate to password.company.com 2) Enter your username 3) Click 'Reset Password' 4) Follow email link..."

**Guideline (Suggestion):**
"Consider using a passphrase composed of unrelated words for easier memorization. Example technique: Combine 4+ random words."

**Baseline (Configuration):**
"Windows GPO: Minimum password length = 12, Complexity = Enabled, Maximum age = 90 days"

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Policy Hierarchy
sections.content:
**From Top to Bottom: P-S-P-G-B**

• **P**olicy = "What" (high-level rules)
• **S**tandard = "Specifically What" (detailed requirements)
• **P**rocedure = "How" (step-by-step)
• **G**uideline = "Suggested How" (recommendations)
• **B**aseline = "Minimum Settings" (configurations)

Think: Policies Set Procedures, Guidelines, and Baselines

sections.title: Common IT Policies
sections.content:
**Security Policies:**
• Information Security Policy
• Acceptable Use Policy
• Access Control Policy
• Password Policy
• Remote Access Policy
• Incident Response Policy
• Data Classification Policy

**Operational Policies:**
• Change Management Policy
• Backup and Recovery Policy
• Asset Management Policy
• Capacity Management Policy

**Governance Policies:**
• IT Governance Policy
• Third-Party Management Policy
• Business Continuity Policy
• Privacy Policy

sections.title: Policy Lifecycle
sections.content:
**1. Development:**
• Identify need (risk, regulation, incident)
• Draft policy content
• Involve stakeholders
• Legal/HR review

**2. Approval:**
• Route through governance
• Obtain appropriate authority signature
• Document approval

**3. Communication:**
• Publish to policy repository
• Announce to organization
• Include in training

**4. Implementation:**
• Develop supporting standards/procedures
• Configure technical controls
• Train personnel

**5. Enforcement:**
• Monitor compliance
• Address violations
• Report exceptions

**6. Review:**
• Annual review at minimum
• Update for changes
• Re-approve as needed

sections.title: Auditing Policies
sections.content:
**Audit Questions:**

**Existence:**
• Are required policies in place?
• Do they cover key areas?

**Quality:**
• Are policies clear and specific enough?
• Are they consistent with each other?
• Do they align with regulations?

**Governance:**
• Are policies approved by appropriate authority?
• Is there a review schedule?
• Is there an owner for each policy?

**Awareness:**
• Are employees aware of policies?
• Have they acknowledged them?
• Is training provided?

**Compliance:**
• Are policies being followed?
• Are violations addressed?
• Are exceptions documented?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Policy hierarchy: Policy → Standard → Procedure → Guideline → Baseline
Policies are mandatory high-level statements; guidelines are flexible recommendations
Policies require formal approval and periodic review (at least annually)
Employees must be made aware of policies through training and acknowledgment
Auditors evaluate policy existence, quality, governance, awareness, and compliance

sections.title: Knowledge Check: Policy vs. Standard vs. Procedure

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of an IT standard?
options: To provide a high-level statement of management's intent regarding IT security.
To provide specific, measurable requirements for implementing a policy.
To provide step-by-step instructions for performing a specific task.
To offer suggestions and best practices for achieving a desired outcome.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Standards define the 'how' by setting specific measurable requirements to ensure policies are implemented effectively. Policies are the 'what', procedures are the 'how-to', and guidelines are suggestions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what is measurable and enforceable.

sections.title: Test Yourself: Policy, Standard, Procedure, Guideline

reveal.question: Define 'IT Policy' in your own words.
reveal.answer: An IT Policy is a high-level statement of management's intent that defines the overall goals and objectives for IT-related activities within an organization. It provides a framework for decision-making and ensures alignment with business objectives and legal/regulatory requirements.

sections.title: Key Differences: Security Policies vs. Operational Policies

rows.label: Primary Focus
values: Protecting the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets.
Ensuring the smooth and efficient operation of IT systems and services.

rows.label: Examples
values: Information Security Policy, Acceptable Use Policy, Access Control Policy
Change Management Policy, Backup and Recovery Policy, Asset Management Policy

rows.label: Impact of Non-Compliance
values: Data breaches, financial loss, reputational damage, legal penalties.
Service disruptions, system failures, inefficiencies, increased costs.

title: Enterprise Architecture
description: Learn enterprise architecture frameworks and their role in IT governance

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Enterprise architecture provides a blueprint for technology investments. Understanding EA helps auditors evaluate whether IT evolves in a controlled, strategic manner.

sections.title: What is Enterprise Architecture?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
A structured framework that describes the structure of an enterprise including its:
• Business processes
• Information flows
• Applications
• Technology infrastructure

**Purpose:**
• Align IT with business
• Reduce complexity and redundancy
• Enable agility
• Guide investment decisions
• Manage change

**Key Concepts:**
• Current state (as-is) architecture
• Target state (to-be) architecture
• Transition roadmap
• Standards and principles
• Governance processes

sections.title: TOGAF Framework
sections.content:
**TOGAF = The Open Group Architecture Framework**

Most widely used EA framework.

**Architecture Development Method (ADM):**

**Preliminary Phase:**
• Prepare and initiate activities
• Define architecture principles

**Phase A: Architecture Vision**
• Develop vision and value proposition

**Phase B: Business Architecture**
• Develop business architecture

**Phase C: Information Systems Architecture**
• Data and Application architectures

**Phase D: Technology Architecture**
• Develop technology architecture

**Phase E: Opportunities and Solutions**
• Identify implementation projects

**Phase F: Migration Planning**
• Transition roadmap

**Phase G: Implementation Governance**
• Oversee implementation

**Phase H: Architecture Change Management**
• Manage changes to architecture

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: TOGAF Phases
sections.content:
**TOGAF ADM: A through H**

Think "ABCD" first for architecture domains:
• A = Vision
• B = Business
• C = Information Systems (Data + Apps)
• D = Technology

Then "EFGH" for implementation:
• E = Evaluate opportunities
• F = plan route Forward (migration)
• G = Govern implementation
• H = Handle changes

sections.title: EA Governance
sections.content:
**Architecture Review Board (ARB):**

**Purpose:**
• Review architectural proposals
• Ensure compliance with standards
• Grant exceptions when justified
• Manage architecture evolution

**Composition:**
• Chief Architect (chair)
• Domain architects
• Security representative
• Business representatives

**Inputs to ARB:**
• Project proposals
• Architecture exception requests
• Technology introduction requests
• Standards updates

**Outputs:**
• Approval/rejection
• Architectural guidance
• Exception documentation
• Standards updates

sections.title: Auditing Enterprise Architecture
sections.content:
**Audit Areas:**

**EA Program:**
• Is there an EA function?
• Is it adequately resourced?
• Is leadership commitment evident?

**Architecture Content:**
• Is current state documented?
• Is target state defined?
• Are roadmaps in place?
• Are standards defined?

**Governance:**
• Is there an Architecture Review Board?
• Are projects reviewed for compliance?
• Are exceptions documented?

**Effectiveness:**
• Is architecture followed in projects?
• Is technical debt managed?
• Is architecture updated for changes?
• Is there value from EA investments?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: EA provides a blueprint spanning Business, Data, Application, and Technology domains
EA bridges current state to target state through roadmaps
TOGAF is the most widely used EA framework with an 8-phase ADM
Architecture Review Board governs architectural decisions and exceptions
Auditors evaluate EA program existence, content, governance, and effectiveness

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST important purpose of Enterprise Architecture (EA)?
options: To implement the latest technology trends within the organization.
To align IT strategy with business goals and objectives.
To reduce the IT budget by standardizing hardware and software.
To ensure all IT projects are completed on time and within budget.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Aligning IT with business goals is the primary purpose of EA. While the other options might be benefits, they are secondary to the overarching strategic alignment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core value that EA provides to the organization.

reveal.question: What is the primary goal of Phase A: Architecture Vision in the TOGAF Architecture Development Method (ADM)?
reveal.answer: To develop the architecture vision and value proposition for the enterprise architecture project. This involves defining the scope, stakeholders, and high-level business requirements.

sections.title: Preliminary Phase vs. Architecture Vision (Phase A)

rows.label: Focus
values: Preparing and initiating architecture development activities; defining architecture principles.
Developing the architecture vision and value proposition; defining scope and high-level requirements.

rows.label: Key Activities
values: Defining the architecture governance framework; identifying stakeholders; securing commitment.
Creating a high-level vision of the future state; defining business scenarios; identifying key business drivers.

rows.label: Deliverables
values: Architecture principles; architecture repository; governance framework.
Architecture vision document; statement of architecture work; stakeholder map.

title: IT Human Resource Management
description: Understand IT staffing, skills management, and personnel security

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: People are IT's most important asset—and potentially its greatest risk. Proper HR management ensures the right skills are available and security risks are managed.

sections.title: IT HR Management Areas
sections.content:
**Workforce Planning:**
• Skills inventory
• Demand forecasting
• Capacity planning
• Vendor/contractor strategy

**Recruitment:**
• Job descriptions
• Skills requirements
• Hiring process
• Background checks

**Development:**
• Training programs
• Certification support
• Career paths
• Performance management

**Retention:**
• Compensation
• Work environment
• Career growth
• Recognition

**Separation:**
• Offboarding process
• Access revocation
• Knowledge transfer
• Exit interviews

sections.title: Personnel Security Controls
sections.content:
**Pre-Employment:**
• Background checks (criminal, credit, employment)
• Reference verification
• Education verification
• Drug screening (where permitted)
• Non-disclosure agreements

**During Employment:**
• Security awareness training
• Policy acknowledgment
• Acceptable use agreements
• Access reviews
• Monitoring (with disclosure)

**Termination:**
• Immediate access revocation
• Return of equipment
• Exit interview
• Remind of ongoing obligations
• Badge and key collection

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Hiring Security
sections.content:
**Before They Start: BRAND**

• **B**ackground check
• **R**eference check
• **A**greements signed (NDA, AUP)
• **N**eed-to-know access only
• **D**ocument everything

Remember: BRAND new employees need BRAND security controls!

sections.title: Critical Role Coverage
sections.content:
**Key Person Dependencies:**

Risk: Over-reliance on individuals who hold critical knowledge.

**Mitigation Strategies:**

**Cross-Training:**
• Multiple people can perform each role
• Reduces single point of failure
• Documented procedures help

**Succession Planning:**
• Identify successors for key roles
• Develop successors proactively
• Document institutional knowledge

**Mandatory Vacations:**
• Forces cross-training
• Can detect fraud (absent employee)
• Required in some industries (banking)

**Job Rotation:**
• Rotates responsibilities periodically
• Develops breadth of skills
• Reduces collusion opportunities

sections.title: Auditing IT HR
sections.content:
**Audit Areas:**

**Hiring:**
• Are background checks performed?
• Are they appropriate for role sensitivity?
• Are NDAs and agreements in place?

**Access:**
• Is access provisioned based on role?
• Is segregation of duties enforced?
• Are access reviews performed?

**Training:**
• Is security awareness training provided?
• Are role-specific skills developed?
• Are certifications maintained?

**Separation:**
• Is access revoked timely upon termination?
• Is equipment returned?
• Is the process documented?

**Coverage:**
• Are critical roles identified?
• Is cross-training in place?
• Is succession planning done?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IT HR covers planning, recruitment, development, retention, and separation
Segregation of duties prevents fraud and errors by separating conflicting functions
Personnel security includes pre-employment checks, ongoing controls, and proper termination
Critical role risks are mitigated through cross-training, succession planning, and job rotation
Mandatory vacations can both force cross-training and help detect fraud

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step when conducting workforce planning for the IT department?
options: A. Developing detailed job descriptions for all IT positions.
B. Performing a skills inventory to identify current IT staff capabilities.
C. Forecasting the demand for IT services based on business objectives.
D. Establishing a vendor/contractor strategy to supplement internal resources.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Forecasting demand (C) is the most important initial step. It determines the organization's future IT needs, which then informs skills inventory, job descriptions, and vendor strategies. Understanding the 'why' (demand) precedes the 'how' (skills, descriptions, vendors).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what needs to be known BEFORE you can determine the right skills, people, or contracts to have in place.

reveal.question: List at least four pre-employment personnel security controls that an organization should implement.
reveal.answer: Acceptable answers include: Background checks (criminal, credit, employment), Reference verification, Education verification, Drug screening (where permitted), Non-disclosure agreements.

rows.label: Purpose
values: To provide employees with specific skills and knowledge needed for their current roles and organizational goals.
To assist employees in obtaining industry-recognized certifications, enhancing their expertise and credibility.

rows.label: Focus
values: Job-specific tasks and responsibilities, company policies, and internal processes.
Broader industry best practices, standards, and technologies as defined by certification bodies.

rows.label: Outcome
values: Improved job performance, increased efficiency, and better alignment with organizational objectives.
Enhanced professional development, increased marketability, and validation of skills.

title: IT Investment and Portfolio Management
description: Learn how organizations evaluate, prioritize, and manage IT investments

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IT investments consume significant resources. Effective portfolio management ensures investments deliver value and align with strategy.

sections.title: IT Portfolio Management
sections.content:
**What is IT Portfolio Management?**
Managing IT investments as a portfolio to optimize value and manage risk.

**Portfolio Types:**

**Application Portfolio:**
• Inventory of all applications
• Health and risk assessment
• Rationalization decisions

**Project Portfolio:**
• Active and proposed projects
• Prioritization and selection
• Resource allocation

**Asset Portfolio:**
• Hardware and infrastructure
• Lifecycle management
• Replacement planning

**Benefits:**
• Strategic alignment
• Optimized resource use
• Risk visibility
• Consistent prioritization
• Better governance

sections.title: Investment Evaluation Methods
rows: Net Present Value: Discounted cash flows
Internal Rate of Return: Discount rate where NPV=0
Total Cost of Ownership: Full lifecycle costs

sections.title: Business Case Development
sections.content:
**Business Case Components:**

**Executive Summary:**
• Investment overview
• Key benefits and costs
• Recommendation

**Business Need:**
• Problem or opportunity
• Strategic alignment
• Consequences of inaction

**Options Analysis:**
• Alternatives considered
• Evaluation criteria
• Recommended option rationale

**Financial Analysis:**
• Costs (capital, operating)
• Benefits (quantified where possible)
• NPV, ROI, payback
• Sensitivity analysis

**Risk Assessment:**
• Key risks
• Mitigation strategies
• Contingency plans

**Implementation Plan:**
• Timeline and milestones
• Resources required
• Dependencies

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Business Case
sections.content:
**Business Case: E-BOFRI**

• **E**xecutive Summary - The elevator pitch
• **B**usiness Need - Why are we doing this?
• **O**ptions - What could we do?
• **F**inancial - What does it cost and return?
• **R**isk - What could go wrong?
• **I**mplementation - How will we do it?

sections.title: Benefits Realization
sections.content:
**Benefits Realization Management:**

Ensures promised investment benefits are actually achieved.

**Process:**

**1. Define Benefits:**
• Identify expected benefits
• Quantify where possible
• Define measurement approach

**2. Assign Ownership:**
• Benefits have business owners
• Not just IT responsibility
• Accountable for realization

**3. Track Benefits:**
• Measure during and after project
• Compare to business case
• Report to governance

**4. Realize and Sustain:**
• Take actions to realize benefits
• Address barriers
• Embed changes for sustainability

**Common Failures:**
• Benefits not quantified upfront
• No ownership assigned
• Tracking ends at go-live
• No accountability for shortfalls

sections.title: Portfolio Governance
sections.content:
**Portfolio Governance Activities:**

**Selection:**
• Evaluate proposals against criteria
• Prioritize based on value and strategy
• Balance risk across portfolio

**Authorization:**
• Approve funding and resources
• Define success criteria
• Establish governance oversight

**Monitoring:**
• Track portfolio health
• Monitor individual investments
• Manage dependencies

**Optimization:**
• Reallocate resources as needed
• Cancel underperforming investments
• Accelerate high-value initiatives

**Reporting:**
• Portfolio dashboards
• Investment status
• Benefits tracking

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Portfolio management treats IT investments holistically to optimize value
Investment methods: ROI, NPV, IRR, Payback Period, TCO - each with pros/cons
Business cases must address need, options, financials, risks, and implementation
Benefits realization ensures promised value is actually delivered
Portfolio governance selects, authorizes, monitors, and optimizes investments

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the PRIMARY objective of IT Portfolio Management?
options: To ensure all IT projects are completed on time and within budget.
To align IT investments with organizational goals and optimize value while managing risk.
To provide a detailed inventory of all IT assets within the organization.
To implement the latest technological advancements regardless of business needs.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: IT Portfolio Management focuses on strategically aligning IT investments with business objectives, maximizing value, and mitigating risks. While project management and asset inventory are important, they are not the PRIMARY objective.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the 'big picture' goal of managing IT investments.

reveal.question: Name three key components that should be included in a Business Case.
reveal.answer: Executive Summary, Business Need, Options Analysis (or similar recognized components such as Financial Analysis, Risk Assessment, or Implementation Plan)

rows.label: Content
values: Inventory of all applications, their health, and risk.
Active and proposed projects, their status, and alignment with strategy.

rows.label: Focus
values: Ongoing operational effectiveness and efficiency of existing applications.
Managing and prioritizing new initiatives and investments.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Rationalizing applications, identifying redundancies, and planning for upgrades or replacements.
Selecting the right projects, allocating resources effectively, and tracking progress.
